extra.message: User-controlled 'name' query parameter flows from line 19 into an f-string at line 38, which is then passed to Template(content).render() at line 41. Jinja2 evaluates {{...}} expressions server-side, enabling Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI). An attacker can achieve Remote Code Execution via payloads like {{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()}}.

User-controlled 'name' parameter is directly interpolated into HTML content returned to the browser without sanitization or escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript via the name query parameter (e.g., /?name=<script>alert(1)</script>). The response_class=HTMLResponse means the Content-Type is text/html, so the browser will execute the injected script.

Hardcoded secret values in application configuration. SUPER_SECRET_NAME is set to 'John Ripper' (line 13) and SUPER_SECRET_TOKEN is set to '5u93R53Cr3tT0k3n' (line 14). The FIXME comments indicate these are placeholders intended to come from environment variables. Hardcoded secrets in source code can be exposed via version control, logs, or error messages.

Server makes an outbound HTTP request to config.PUBLIC_IP_SERVICE_URL which is fetched from an environment variable (config.py:11). While the URL is not directly user-controllable via query parameters, the SSTI vulnerability (line 38-41) allows an attacker to execute arbitrary code on the server and redirect this request to internal services, or modify the environment to change the URL. This can be used to scan internal networks, access cloud metadata endpoints, or exploit internal services.

Debug mode is enabled (config.DEBUG = True by default via .env_temp). This is passed to FastAPI on main.py:14 (debug=config.DEBUG). Debug mode exposes detailed error messages, stack traces, and interactive debugger (if using Starlette debug mode) to end users, potentially leaking sensitive information about the application internals.
